text: Lower ankle dorsiflexion flexibility is associated with better running economy in sub-elite male distance runners (r = 0.65, p ≤ 0.05).
Lower standing hip rotation flexibility is associated with better running economy in sub-elite male distance runners (r = 0.53, p ≤ 0.05).
Musculoskeletal inflexibility in the ankle and hip may enhance running economy by increasing storage and return of elastic energy in tendons and muscles.
Musculoskeletal inflexibility may improve running economy by reducing the need for muscle-stabilizing activity during the gait cycle.
Excessive static stretching targeting ankle dorsiflexion and hip rotation may be counterproductive for distance runners seeking to optimize running economy.
